text: Good afternoon. My name is Ann Chung.
I'll be presenting a case from the University of North Carolina.
I'd like to thank SAGES and our moderators for this opportunity to present.
I have no disclosures.
This is a case of malrotation in an adult for which we performed a laparoscopic LADS procedure.
Just a little bit of background.
Intestinal malrotation is a congenital condition caused by a rest of abnormal rotation of the gut during fetal development.
In normal development, the primary intestinal loop is pushed out into the area of the yolk stack and begins a normal rotation of the bowel twisting ninety degrees counterclockwise.
During weeks eight to ten of gestation, the loop then returns into the abdomen, and as it returns, there is an additional rotation of a hundred and eighty degrees counterclockwise.
Any interruption of this pathway can lead to an aberrant location of the intestines, formation of LADS bands, and a shortened mesentery.
This abnormal anatomy can lead to issues with volvulus and ballast ischemia.
There are various anomalies of gut rotation with the most common types being non-rotation and mal-rotation.
In non-rotation, The primary loop of intestine does not rotate when it returns to the abdominal cavity, thus the small bowel is located on the right, the colon is located on the left.
There's less risk of volvulus with this type of variation.
With malrotation, which is what we commonly see clinically, the duodenal jejunal limb is non-rotated.
The surgeon observes the cecum partially rotated and fixed in the upper abdomen by Ladd's bands, which visibly cross the duodenum, causing extrinsic compression and potential obstruction.
In this situation, these patients tend to present commonly with volvulus.
Malrotation is typically diagnosed in childhood, although the incidence of diagnosis in adults is reportedly as high as forty percent.
Adults tend to have a chronic indolent course with intermittent colicky abdominal pain and vomiting.
It is more common in children to present acutely associated with volvulus.
The gold standard for diagnosing malrotation is an upper GI series, but a CT scan is also commonly used.
The treatment for intestinal malrotation is a LAS procedure, which can be performed laparoscopically or open.
The steps for the procedure are as follows.
If there is a volvulus present, then the bowel is rotated counterclockwise.
The surgeon divides the LAS bands connecting the cecum, duodenum, and mesentery, using surgical instruments to carefully separate these structures.
The surgeon performs an appendectomy, removing the appendix as part of the Ladd's procedure.
The surgeon completes the procedure by positioning the small bowel on the right side of the abdominal cavity and the colon on the left side.
Currently, the laparoscopic approach is most commonly undertaken and has been shown to be as safe and effective as the traditional open reproach in both children and adults.
Our case is a fifty-four year old male who presented to our clinic with a history of chronic intermittent abdominal pain and no prior abdominal surgeries.
He underwent a CT scan and was found to have intestinal malrotation with small bowel wall thickening concerning for intermittent volvulus.
Given his symptoms and imaging consistent with malrotation, we offered him a laparoscopic LADS procedure.
This is his CT scan, just a couple of cuts, and you can see that he's got intestinal marrow and he's got some small bowel wall thickening on some of his loops of small bowel.
Upon entering the abdomen, the surgeon identifies the cecum, which is abnormally located in the right upper quadrant.
The surgeon retracts the cecum medially and begins mobilizing the right colon along the white line of Toldt towards the hepatic flexure.
The surgeon identifies the appendix, which is visually observed to be located in a retrocecal position.
During the mobilization for the appendectomy, the surgeon identifies the right ureter positioned laterally to the appendix.
The surgeon carefully laces adhesions surrounding the appendix, with the psoas muscle visible posteriorly in the surgical field.
The appendix is visually positioned over the inferior vena cava and the duodenum, indicating its atypical anatomical location.
The surgeon completes the appendectomy and removes the appendix, placing it into an endocatch bag for extraction.
The surgeon flips the right colon medially, revealing the underlying duodenum.
Adhesions, known as Ladd's bands, are visible extending from the duodenum to the surrounding retroperitoneum and colon, along with interloop adhesions causing kinking of the bowel.
The surgeon divides the adhesions surrounding the appendix using a combination of blunt dissection and electrocautery, with the psoas muscle visible posteriorly.
The surgeon identifies adhesions to the retroperitoneum with the duodenum overlying the inferior vena cava (IVC) and proceeds to dissect these adhesions away.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the right colon by opening the gastrocolic ligament at the mid-transverse colon and dividing it towards the hepatic flexure.
The right colon is completely mobilized and flipped medially, revealing the entire duodenum and the head of the pancreas.

The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and electrocautery dissection to remove additional adhesions between the duodenum and the mesentery, visibly freeing the duodenum from these connective tissues.
The surgical field reveals the head of the pancreas as the surgeon approaches this anatomical structure, following the mobilization of surrounding tissues.
During adhesiolysis, the surgeon identifies a fatty band adhered to the terminal ileum overlying the duodenum, causing obstruction, and proceeds to divide the band.
The surgeon runs the small bowel proximally towards the ligament of Treitz after completely freeing the right colon and duodenum from each other.
The surgeon encounters adhesions between the small bowel and the root of the mesentery while running the bowel, indicating areas where the tissue is adhered and requires careful dissection.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to divide the adhesions between the duodenum and the mesentery, continuing the dissection process.
And again, this is in a patient who has had no prior abdominal surgeries before.
After taking down the adhesions, the surgeon observes the broadening of the mesentery, an important step in the Ladd's procedure.
The video shows the staple line from the appendectomy, indicating the location where the appendix was removed.
The surgeon identifies the colon and small bowel, using the separation of adhesions to clarify the anatomical structures.
After completing the lysis of adhesions, the surgeon positions the small bowel on the right side and the colon in the left upper quadrant of the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon continues to broaden the mesentery, a crucial step in the Ladd's procedure, ensuring the small bowel and colon are positioned correctly.
So the patient tolerated the procedure well.
He was admitted for observation and was discharged on post-operative day one, tolerating a regular diet.
He was seen in clinic for his post-operative appointment about two weeks following surgery and was tolerating a regular diet and having no further episodes of abdominal pain or vomiting.
Thank you for, again, allowing me to present.
I'd be happy to take any questions.
